coredata.dc:title: 2D mesoporous silica-confined CsPbBr3 nanocrystals and N-doped graphene quantum dot: A self-enhanced quaternary composite structures for electrochemiluminescence analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Wei, Jinliu
Chen, Liming
Cai, Xiaoyan
Lai, Wenqiang
Chen, Xi
Cai, Zhixiong
coredata.dc:description: Lead halide perovskites have become a potential candidate as electrochemiluminescence (ECL) emitters owing to their appealing electronic-to-optical merits. It remains extremely challenging, however, to improve stability and enhance charge transfer. Herein, a self-enhanced superstructures was constructed by successively loading N-doped graphene quantum dot (NGQDs) and CsPbBr3 perovskite nanocrystals (PNCs) onto graphene supported two-dimensional mesoporous SiO2 nanosheets (2D mSiO2-G). This special architecture ensures improved stability and accelerated charge transport, leading to efficient self-enhanced ECL between NGQDs and PNCs in a confined mesoporous structure. Additionally, using molecular imprinting (MIP) as a protective barrier, an ECL sensor with high affinity for Ochratoxin A (OTA) detection was developed, which expressed the widest linear range of 10−5 ng/mL to 1.0 ng/mL and the lowest detection limit of 0.2 pg/mL. This work catches a glimpse of a new generation of desirable perovskite-based ECL emitters, which would be beneficial for its further application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Self-enhanced electrochemiluminescence
Perovskite nanocrystals
Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dot
2D mesoporous SiO2 nanosheets
Ochratoxin A detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the OTA-imprinted ECL sensor
Construction of MIP-ECL sensor for OTA determination

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of the template graphene; (B) TEM image of mSiO2-GO nanosheets and (C) the closing look; (D) SEM image of mSiO2-GO mSiO2-GO nanosheets; (E) TEM images of NGQDs@mSiO2-GO (Inset: HRTEM); (F) TEM images of PNCs-NGQDs@mSiO2-GO (Inset: HRTEM); (G) HAADF-STEM and elemental mapping images of the PNCs-NGQDs@mSiO2-GO (C in NGQDs; Cs, Pb, and Br in PNCs).

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of NGQDs (black), mSiO2-G (pink), PNCs (red), and PNCs-NGQDs@mSiO2-G (blue); (B) PL emission spectra of NGQDs (magnified 1000 times) (black), PNCs (blue), and PNCs-NGQDs@mSiO2-G (red); (C) PL evolutions of PNCs (black) and PNCs-NGQDs@mSiO2-G (red) n-hexane dispersion stored in reagent bottles in 20 days; (D) ECL evolutions of PNCs-modified (black) and PNCs-NGQDs@mSiO2-G (red)-modified GCEs soaked in deionized water for 10 h.

(A) Profiles of ECL-potentials and (inset) enlarged signals of different modified GCEs; (B) Schematic diagram of the possible self-enhanced ECL reaction mechanism; (C) ECL responses and (D) EIS plots in PBS of PNCs-NGQDs@mSiO2-G/GCE (a, violet), MIP/PNCs-NGQDs@mSiO2-G/GCE (b, red) and after elution (c, green), and then incubation in 0.1 ng/mL OTA solution (d, blue).

(A) The ECL curves and ECL intensity−potential curves for different concentrations of OTA obtained by the proposed MIP sensor; (B) The corresponding calibration curve; (C) The stability of the ECL MIP sensor; (D) Specificity and selectivity of the ECL MIP sensor (the concentration of each toxin was 1.0 ng/mL).
Schematic illustration of the fabrication and application of the self-enhanced MIP-ECL sensor for OTA detection.
Detection of OTA in wheat flour by the MIP-ECL sensor.

head.ce:title: 2D mesoporous silica-confined CsPbBr3 nanocrystals and N-doped graphene quantum dot: A self-enhanced quaternary composite structures for electrochemiluminescence analysis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Environment, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou, 363000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Environment, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou 363000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Zhangzhou Affiliated Hospital of Fujian Medical University, Zhangzhou, 363000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Zhangzhou Affiliated Hospital of Fujian Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Zhangzhou Affiliated Hospital of Fujian medical University, Zhangzhou 363000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xiamen University, Xiamen, 361005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xiamen University, Xiamen 361005, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lead halide perovskites have become a potential candidate as electrochemiluminescence (ECL) emitters owing to their appealing electronic-to-optical merits. It remains extremely challenging, however, to improve stability and enhance charge transfer. Herein, a self-enhanced superstructures was constructed by successively loading N-doped graphene quantum dot (NGQDs) and CsPbBr3 perovskite nanocrystals (PNCs) onto graphene supported two-dimensional mesoporous SiO2 nanosheets (2D mSiO2-G). This special architecture ensures improved stability and accelerated charge transport, leading to efficient self-enhanced ECL between NGQDs and PNCs in a confined mesoporous structure. Additionally, using molecular imprinting (MIP) as a protective barrier, an ECL sensor with high affinity for Ochratoxin A (OTA) detection was developed, which expressed the widest linear range of 10−5 ng/mL to 1.0 ng/mL and the lowest detection limit of 0.2 pg/mL. This work catches a glimpse of a new generation of desirable perovskite-based ECL emitters, which would be beneficial for its further application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: As a crucial semiconductor, lead halide perovskite (APbX3, A = CH3NH3 +, (NH2)2CH+, or Cs+; X = Cl−, Br−, or I−) materials has triggered a gold rush in some fields, such as solar cells, light-emitting diodes, lasers, and photodetectors. Additionally, colloidal CsPbX3 (X = Cl, Br, I) perovskite nanocrystals (PNCs) have been proved to be a promising electrochemiluminescence (ECL) emitter recently (Cai et al., 2018; Cao and Zhu, 2021; Cao et al., 2020). Since PNCs are highly hygroscopic and chemically unstable when exposed to the environment, their typical ECL applications are severely restricted. Therefore, exploring a stabilization strategy for PNCs is critical to the advancement of ECL. To improve the stability of PNCs, many efforts have been devoted to embedding into an inert matrix or surface modification (Loiudice et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2018). As an easily available inorganic material, SiO2's inherent chemical stability, high surface area and tunable porosity, make it an ideal supporter for constructing highly durable nanocomposites. Thus, many researchers are committed to stabilizing CsPbX3 QDs with SiO2 (Huang et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2016; Zhong et al., 2018). A porosity in SiO2 is highly desirable for the in situ loading of PNCs. On the other side, ECL is a light-emitting process in which excited substances are generated in the vicinity of electrodes through chemical reactions. PNCs can be stabilized by the protective matrices from the external environment, but their charge and mass transport capabilities are compromised, which is critical to ECL efficiency. Therefore, to find compromise between ECL efficiency and structural stability, exploring a suitable construction remains a challenge.

A great deal of progress has been made in the development of advanced sensors thanks to carbon-based quantum dots, primarily carbon and graphene quantum dots (CQD and GQD), because of their excellent optical, electrical, and chemical properties, especially the application in the area of electrochemiluminescence (ECL) sensing. Particularly, N-doped GQDs (NGQDs) with functional groups (amines) have recently attracted a lot of attention as potential ECL coreactants (Carrara et al., 2017; Fan et al., 2022; Qi et al., 2017). For this reason, it is expected that suitable NGQDs with sufficient amines could serve as a promising alternative to volatile and harmful alkylamines as a coreactant for PNCs.

Solid-state ECL by immobilizing the ECL emitters onto the electrode surface shows large number superiorities such as enhanced stability and ECL intensity, saved amount of costly ECL reagent, simplified instrument compared with the ECL system in solution phase (Li et al., 2009; Yu et al., 2008; Zhu et al., 2008). Two-dimensional nanomaterials are well known for their ability to fabricate multilayered membranes by crosslinking nanosheets, which is of great interest for fabricating solid-state ECL sensors. On the other side, it is well known that mesoporous materials are ideal carriers for guest molecules, due to their large specific surface area and facile surface functionalization (Kankala et al., 2020; Niculescu, 2020; Wu et al., 2011). Mesoporous materials, when combined with 2D structure, can provide abundant active sites, thus increasing the probability of analytes and active sites contacting each other to improve the detection sensitivity. However, applications of mesoporous silica in sensing based solid-state ECL approach remain rare, partly due to the intrinsic properties: electronic insulators. Since graphene has been extensively studied as an electron-transport matrix, its incorporation can enhance charge-separation facilitate charge-transport.

Take above factors into account, herein, 2D mesoporous SiO2-G nanosheets were fabricated via in situ hydrolysis and condensation of tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) on the surfaces of graphene oxide (GO), and then used as ideal carrier to load NGQDs and PNCs. The graphene layer is acting as template for mesoporous SiO2 to form tightly mesoporous SiO2-GO nanosheets, and behaves as an electron transfer medium to enhance the electron transport ability. As a mesoporous silicon template, graphene acts as a medium to enhance electron transport and assemble tightly packed mesoporous SiO2 nanosheets. Thank to large specific surface area from the mesoporous and 2D structural feature of SiO2, which benefits the dispersion of PNCs and the enrichment of NGQDs, thus the amount of NGQDs and PNCs loaded greatly increased. In this elaborate structure, mSiO2-G nanosheets acts as a reliable loader to protect the encapsulated PNCs from the external conditions to a great extent, affording improved stability. Furthermore, NGQDs provide sufficient amines and surface oxygen (e.g., alcohols) to be the coreactant, and improve the charge-transfer. Meanwhile, the confined structure brings PNCs and the surrounding NGQDs together that facilitating charge-transfer and undertaking minor energy losses. Accordingly, significantly improved ECL performance in terms of stability and efficiency is achieved from the PNCs-NGQDs@mSiO2-G. Integrated with Ochratoxin A (OTA) MIP membrane with high affinity, selectivity and insulation, a novel solid-state MIP-ECL sensor was proposed by quaternary PNCs-NGQDs@mSiO2-G nanosheets aiming to conduct a contamination assessment of OTA.
See supporting information for detail.

NGQDs were prepared by treating 3D N-doped graphene aerogel (NGA) as reported in our previous work with strong acid (Lin et al., 2015; Song et al., 2014). The mSiO2-G were prepared according to previous report with trifling revise (Shang et al., 2014). NGQDs@SiO2-G was prepared by electrostatic interaction between positively charged NH2-mSiO2-G and negatively charged NGQDs (see supporting information for detail).

PNCs-NGQDs@mSiO2-G nanosheets as ECL emitters were synthesized by the procedure showed in upper part of Scheme 1 . PNCs-NGQDs@mSiO2-G was prepared via growing PNCs in situ in the mesoporous pores of mSiO2-G with reprecipitation–encapsulation method (see supporting information for detail).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the OTA-imprinted ECL sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The schematic illustration of the PNCs-NGQDs@mSiO2-G based ECL sensor for the detection of OTA is shown in Scheme 1 (see supporting information for detail).

We pretreated wheat flour samples for Ochratoxin A analysis by immersing 5.0 g of ground cereal in 10 mL of 50% methanol. For analysis, the supernatant was separated from the mixture after 20 min of ultrasonic treatment and centrifugation (10 min, 10 000 rpm).

As described in the previous report, the NGQDs were prepared with a little modification (Lin et al., 2015). Especially, the present NGQDs can be well-distributed in water without ultrasonic operation. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images showed uniform, monodisperse NGQDs with an average diameter of (2.5 ± 0.3) nm (Fig. S1). Fig. 1 A, B, C showed the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of GO, mSiO2-GO nanosheet, and close-look of mSiO2-GO nanosheets. The TEM image in Fig. 1A illustrates the ultra-thin 2D-structural GO consisting of gossamer nanosheets with long wrinkles (Cai et al., 2015). The TEM image of mSiO2-GO in Fig. 1B showed that mesoporous silica coating uniformly covers on the GO template forming dense structure with thicker morphology, which was also confirmed by the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image (Fig. 1D). In comparison to GO, the mSiO2-GO nanocomposites possessed a more compact structure by sandwiching graphene sheets between layers of mSiO2, which fabricated by hydrolysis and condensation of pre-adsorbed TEOS on GO sheets. By combining the unique features of 2D and mesoporous structures, these 2D mesoporous materials can not only tune electric structures, but also increase the accessible surface sites of 2D materials (Ai et al., 2021). A close-look of mSiO2-GO nanosheets displayed large number of mesopores with abundant cavities (Fig.1C and Fig. S2), which can provide lots of space for enwrapping other 0D nanocomposites due to its large specific surface area. By using Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) gas adsorption method, the mesoporous structure of mSiO2-G was confirmed and suggested a surface area of 1015 m2/g and an average Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) pore diameter of 3.2 nm (Fig. S3). The above results indicated the successful preparation of mSiO2-GO with hierarchical porosity. After NGQDs were immobilized on mSiO2-GO nanosheets through electrostatic adsorption to the modified APTES (Luo et al., 2019b), numerous black dots can be observed on the surface of mSiO2-GO from TEM (Fig. 1E), and it can be found that the resulting NGQDs@mSiO2-GO were relatively homogeneous. Besides, no obvious aggregation of NGQDs were found, showing that most of the NGQDs were confined to the mSiO2.

As our previous work, in the cavity of mSiO2 were infiltrated by PNCs precursor via capillary forces, and forming PNCs in situ (Huang et al., 2019). Then PNCs nanocrystals that newly formed in cavities of NGQDs@mSiO2-GO were observed on mSiO2 from Fig. 1F, and it can be identified the lattice distance of 0.29 nm (Fig. 1F inset). For further verification, as illustrated in Fig. 1G by high angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) and its corresponding elemental mapping images, the overlapped Cs, Pb and Br elements are uniformly distributed, confirming the successful formation of CsPbBr3. These results demonstrate that PNCs are integrated into NGQDs@mSiO2-GO substrate in situ, forming the PNCs-NGQDs@mSiO2-GO quaternary nanocomposite successfully. The strategy of integrating the PNCs onto mSiO2-GO that possessing high surface area is beneficial to reduce the aggregation state than that of same amounts of PNCs, thus increasing the opportunities for contact between PNCs and NGQDs to some extent.

The photophysical properties were further investigated. From Fig. S4A inset, the solution of NGQDs with a tawny appearance emits pale green fluorescence under the UV lamp (365 nm) with a comparatively low quantum yield (2.4% quinine sulfate as reference). Fig. S4A showed a typical photoluminescence (PL) and UV–vis absorption spectra of the NGQDs. As shown in Fig. S4B, NGQDs displayed excitation-dependent character with the maximum emission peak at 545 nm when the excitation wavelength was set at 463 nm. Although a high luminous efficiency of NGQDs is not achieved in this work, it is recognized that the introduction of N atoms into benzene ring atoms can signally regulate the charge spin and distribution of the carbon atoms, thus enhancing the capacity of catalysis of active centers on the NGQDs surface (Lin et al., 2015). Moreover, the sufficient amines and surface oxygen in these NGQDs allow them not only to stabilize radical species but also to facilitate reversible reactions that may involve radical formation, which would benefit the ECL efficiency as a coreactant (Qi et al., 2017). The full-scan X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum (Fig. S5) further showed the three main peaks, confirming the presence of elements of C1s, N1s, and O1s in NGCDs, respectively. The UV–vis absorption spectrums are displayed in Fig. 2 A. NGQDs showed weak absorption, probably as a result of molecular vibration and rotation of amines and surface oxygen groups, leading to nonradiative transition. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of mSiO2-GO showed that the peak at ∼230 nm should be corresponding to π-π* transitions of aromatic CC, and a well-defined absorption shoulder peak at ∼270 nm that related to the n-π* electronic transition of non-bonding electrons of oxygen-containing functional groups, exhibiting similar profile to GO (Dong et al., 2012). The absorption pattern of NGQDs@mSiO2-GO was consistent with that of mSiO2-GO (not show). The UV–vis absorption spectra of PNCs started at ∼543 nm, and reached the extremum at ∼516 nm, and then maintained strong absorption. As for the PNCs-NGQDs@mSiO2-G, the absorption peak was similar to the PNCs with improved absorption and typical cut-off feature at ∼516 nm, indicating the successful fabrication of the composite. The PL spectra were depicted in Fig. 2B. In addition, NGQDs showed weak PL emission peak at 540 nm (Fig. S4A). After NGQDs electrostatic bonding to 2D mSiO2-G, NGQDs@mSiO2-GO showed a negligible PL emission, might due to the transfer of the excited energy to graphene. With the in situ growth of PNCs, PNCs-NGQDs@mSiO2-G retained almost the same PL characteristics the individual PNCs, indicating the successful attachment of PNCs and unaffected optical property. Fig. S6 also showed the bright-field and fluorescence photographs of PNCs-NGQDs@mSiO2-G nanosheets. Accordingly, the successful preparation of PNCs-NGQDs@mSiO2-G was demonstrated the photophysical properties.

The stability of PNCs-NGQDs@mSiO2-G is further examined with respect to external environment (Fig. 2C). We record the PL decay of PNCs and PNCs-NGQDs@mSiO2-G in the n-hexane dispersion at 4 °C for twenty days, then the quaternary nanocomposite remains 72.1% compared to the initial PL intensity, while that of PNCs is almost extinct, which implies the considerably enhanced air stability of the nanostructure. The water stability is also investigated by recording their ECL intensity changes in deionized water (Fig. 2D). The PNCs-NGQDs@mSiO2-G modified GCE can maintain 91.8% and 62.1% of the original ECL intensity after 3 h and 10 h, respectively, while the PNCs modified GCE decay to non ECL signal rapidly. The improved stability would owe to the protection of mSiO2-G, which serve as a robust carrier for decelerating the diffusion of O2 and H2O to internal PNCs, and the 3 h stability in water (remains above 90% ECL intensity) was stable enough to ensures the ECL study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of MIP-ECL sensor for OTA determination
ce:section.ce:para:
The ECL of the self-enhanced PNCs-NGQDs@mSiO2-G luminophore was explored to help elucidate the mechanisms in PBS. As shown in Fig. 3 A, ECL responses for mSiO2-G and NGQDs@mSiO2-G are almost nonexistent (curve f, e), while ECL signal for PNCs@mSiO2-G is very low (curve d), perhaps as a result of the coreaction of surface ligands or reactive oxygen species. Due to the abundant amines content of NGQDs, the ECL response PNCs@mSiO2-G of containing 0.10 mg/mL of them increases clearly, demonstrating their efficacy as coreaction agents in the ECL process (Fan et al., 2022). Upon upload NGQDs and PNCs onto the mSiO2-G simultaneously forming PNCs-NGQDs@mSiO2-G, a self-enhanced luminophore without any extra coreactant featured a shorter electron-transfer distance and low energy loss, resulting in a stronger ECL response (curve a). However, a weaken and positive-shifted ECL signal was acquired for MIP PNCs-NGQDs@mSiO2-G modified GCE (curve c), mainly because of the poor electrical conductivity of MIP membrane. The MIP membrane does, however, provide an effective protection to ensure that ECL signals are more stable, as shown in Fig. S7. In addition, due to the relaxation of the injected charges caused by the unavoidable surface defects, ECL spectra peak generates bathochromic shift slightly and become broadened, comparing to the PL spectra (Fig. S8) (Sun et al., 2009). Thus, a possible self-enhanced mechanism resulting from intra-reactions between PNCs and NGQDs coreactant is depicted as below (Li et al., 2019; Luo et al., 2019a):
(1) PNCs-NGQDs@mSiO2-G – e
-
→ PNCs-NGQDs+•@mSiO2-G
(2) PNCs-NGQDs+•@mSiO2-G → PNCs-NGQDs•@mSiO2-G + H +
(3) PNCs-NGQDs•@mSiO2-G – e
- → PNCs + -NGQDs•@mSiO2-G
(4) PNCs + -NGQDs•@mSiO2-G → PNCs*-NGQDs@mSiO2-G
(5) PNCs*-NGQDs@mSiO2-G → PNCs-NGQDs@mSiO2-G + hv

Briefly, NGQDs are first electrooxidized to NGQDs+•, then NGQD+• is transformed into radical intermediate NGQDs• with highly reductive by deprotonating the available amines in it Meanwhile, PNCs are electrooxidized to PNCs + . The electron-transfer from NGQDs• to PNCs + by intramolecular reaction to produced excited PNCs* for ECL emission, which starts from +0.60 V (Fig. 3B). In these circumstances, the high ECL efficiency of the nanocomposite could be attributed to the following factors. First, NGQDs provides available amino groups as intra-coreactant, and enhances the charge transport capability together with graphene. Second, the adjacency of PNCs and NGQDs confined on an individual nanocarrier could shorten the electron-transfer path during the ECL reactions. In this way, charge and mass transportation are not affected by inert resistance. Third, the similar oxidation potential of PNCs and NGQDs benefits the generation of electrooxidized spices simultaneously, reducing the possibility of radical decomposition and displaying a self-enhanced mode.

The MIP-ECL sensors were also characterized using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Meanwhile, the corresponding ECL signal was measured. The GCE surface was modified with PNCs-NGQDs@mSiO2-G nanosheets displayed a smallest Rct value (Fig. 3D, curve a) and a prominent ECL peak appeared concurrently (Fig. 3C, curve a), emphasizing the relatively good conductivity. Subsequently, the MIP-coated GCE present a remarkable resistance (Fig. 3D, curve b), which caused by the insulated MIP film that blocks charge transport, thus resulting in large attenuation of the ECL signal (Fig. 3C, curve b). Upon elution by ethanol, molecule cavities were formed by the washing off of template OTA molecules. As a result of these cavities, electrons and mass can be transferred easier, resulting in a lower resistance (Fig. 3D, curve c) and an increased ECL signal (Fig. 3C, curve c). The results demonstrated that it is feasible to develop a MIP ECL method for OTA detection. In addition, after incubating the eluted MIP modified electrode in the target OTA solution, the impedance value increased significantly (Fig. 3D, curve d), which is due to the recombination of OTA molecules with the imprinted cavities, ultimately leading to poor electrical conductivity and an obvious reduced ECL signal (Fig. 3C, curve d). Accordingly, a self-enhanced PNCs-NGQDs@mSiO2-G luminophore is successfully incorporated into the MIP sensor for OTA detection based on these results.

Furthermore, several experimental parameters, comprising MIP film formation time under UV light, elution time and rebinding time were explored to achieve good analytical performance. Firstly, the influence of the UV light irradiation time on the ECL signal of MIP/PNCs-NGQDs@mSiO2-G was optimized. Precursor mixture of imprinting polymer was dispersed on the pre-modified PNCs-NGQDs@mSiO2-G GCE to be UV crosslinked at different irradiation times. As shown in Fig. S9A, due to the not yet formed MIP film, short irradiation times were undesirable. However, the ECL signal of MIP/PNCs-NGQDs@mSiO2-G/GCE decreased to a steady state after 12 min irradiation, indicating that MIP film formation by photopolymerization reaction was successful. Therefore, 12 min was selected as ultraviolet light irradiation. Besides, the elution time and rebinding time were investigated. As illustrated in Fig. S9B, the ECL emission raised with the increasing elution time and reached its maximum around 8 min, indicating that the template molecules of OTA were nearly eluted to form molecular vacancies, thereby increasing the ECL intensity. Accordingly, the optimal elution time of 8 min was chosen. Inversely, with the increasing time of incubation in OTA solution, the ECL signal of pre-eluted GCE gradually decayed and steadied after 10 min since the specific recombination between specific vacancies and the target molecules (Fig. S9C). Therefore, the optimal incubation time was 10 min.

A series of standard solutions containing different concentrations of OTA was used to obtain the standard curve for the proposed MIP-ECL sensors under the optimal conditions. As shown in Fig. 4 A, with increasing concentrations of target, the ECL signal of the sensor gradually decreased, which was explained by the cavities left by elution acting as recognition and binding sites. This was caused by the electron transfer channel being blocked after being reoccupied by the target molecules (Wu et al., 2019). Fig. 4B depicted the calibration curve for ECL sensors detecting OTA by plotting ECL response versus appropriate target concentrations. In detail, the logarithm of OTA concentration was well correlated to the ECL intensity, which ranges between 10−5 ng/mL and 1.0 ng/mL (I
ECL = 159.1–243.3 lg c , R
2 = 0.994). The standard deviation (SD) value of the blank ECL signal was evaluated by ten times to obtain the limit of detection (LOD), whose value of 3SD/slope was calculated to 0.2 pg/mL. Fig. 4C showed the ECL response under consecutive CV scanning for 15 cycles, which exhibited a satisfactory stability with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 1.63%. Fig. 4D examined the specificity of MIP-ECL sensors using other typical mycotoxins for interference tests, including zearalenone (ZEA), vomitoxin (DON), AFB1 and AFB2. A marked reduction in ECL intensity was observed when the sensor was incubated with OTA, whereas a negligible change occurred when the sensor was incubated with the same concentration of other interference, which demonstrated high selectivity for OTA. Then, OTA in wheat flour was determined using the standard addition method to verify the potential application of MIP-ECL sensor for real samples detection. A recovery test was performed by spiking samples with standard concentrations of OTA (1.0, 0.5, and 0.1 ng/mL). As shown in Table 1 , recoveries ranging from 98 to 112% with RSD at 1.5 ∼ 3.2% were obtained for wheat flour samples. These results confirmed the high reliability of the PNCs-NGQDs@mSiO2-G based MIP-ECL sensor for OTA detection. Hence, it is suitable for analyzing real samples.

We propose the strategy by successively loading NGQDs and PNCs onto 2D mSiO2-G for self-enhanced ECL, providing high efficiency and stability. Besides the graphene support's high surface area and conductivity, the confined architecture of mSiO2 layers is also responsible for the enhanced performance, which provide an isolated and abundant confined space for NGQDs and PNCs to land in situ, thus dramatically improving stability. Aside from being an effective intra co-reactant with sufficient amines, NGQDs are also capable of improving the conductivity and charge-transfer efficiency of the nanocomposite; As NGQDs and PNCs are combined to fabricate a novel self-enhanced complex, the electron-transfer distance will be shortened and the intensity of ECL will be greatly enhanced. With these superiorities in mind, this work also proposes a MIP-ECL sensing platform based on this material that achieves ultrasensitive contamination assessments of OTA in wheat flour samples. Besides the high affinity and selectivity, integrated with MIP membrane can also insulate the aqueous environment from PNCs to improve the stability. In addition to providing a novel approach for the design of promising alternatives to conventional semiconductor ECL emitters, this work also expands halide perovskites' application prospects in the ECL field.

Jinliu Wei: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation. Liming Chen: Methodology, Data curation. Xiaoyan Cai: Validation, Resources. Wenqiang Lai: Resources, Project administration. Xi Chen: Writing – review & editing. Zhixiong Cai: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant NO. 21904055), and the Nature Science Foundation of Fujian province (Grant NO. 2020J05164) and the Education-Science Research Project for Young and Middle-aged Teachers of Fujian (Grant NO. JAT210282).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Colloidal CsPbBr3 perovskite nanocrystal films as electrochemiluminescence emitters in aqueous solutions
